course_info.title: Business Capstone Project
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Capstone Kickoff, Client Engagement, and Team Formation
learning_objectives: Understand the capstone project scope, milestones, and evaluation criteria
Form balanced consulting teams based on complementary skill sets and functional backgrounds
Begin client engagement with a structured intake process
readings: Minto, The Pyramid Principle, Introduction and Ch. 1-3
Rasiel, The McKinsey Way, selected chapters
Sample capstone reports from previous semesters (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: The business capstone is your opportunity to demonstrate everything you have learned in the BA program. Over 15 weeks, your team will work with a real or simulated client, diagnose a business challenge, develop strategic recommendations grounded in analysis, and deliver a professional consulting engagement. This course approaches capstone the way top management consulting firms approach engagements: with a defined scope, hypothesis-driven analysis, MECE structuring, rigorous financial modeling, and executive-level communication.
key_concepts: Milestone-driven consulting: initiation, diagnosis, strategy development, financial modeling, recommendations, debrief
Client engagement: intake meeting, scope agreement, working norms, confidentiality, and deliverable schedule
MECE structuring: mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive problem decomposition
Team roles: engagement lead, analyst, modeler, presenter; functional strengths from finance, marketing, operations, strategy
Scope management: fitting ambition to 15-week timeline and team capability, explicit exclusions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Review three previous capstone projects: one that succeeded by scoping tightly, one that pivoted after week 4 when the initial hypothesis collapsed, and one that overscoped and produced shallow recommendations. Analyze lessons from each and apply them to team scoping discussions.
setup_requirements: Client (real or simulated) assigned or selected
Project management tool configured (Asana, Notion, or GitHub Projects)

review.value: The smallest scope that delivers real client value in 15 weeks beats the ambitious scope that produces a shallow report. Pick a specific problem your team can actually solve, not a vague strategic question that cannot be resolved in the timeline

The client's needs, not your curiosity, define success. Every deliverable should answer a question the client actually has and could act on. Intellectual exercises unconnected to client needs do not count as consulting

class_schedule.topic: Milestone 1 Review: Project Charter and Diagnostic Plan
learning_objectives: Present a complete project charter with problem statement, scope, and working hypothesis
Demonstrate a diagnostic plan with specific analytical questions and data requirements
Receive and incorporate faculty and peer feedback on approach and feasibility
readings: Rumelt, Good Strategy Bad Strategy, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: The charter is your engagement's foundation. A thorough charter prevents costly direction changes by forcing clarity on the client's problem, your hypothesis, and the evidence you will need to prove or disprove it. Consulting firms call this the 'storyboard first' approach: outline the report you hope to deliver before starting the analysis, so each analytical step serves a purpose.
key_concepts: Problem statement: a specific client question framed clearly enough to answer
Working hypothesis: an initial answer the analysis will test (hypothesis-driven consulting)
Diagnostic plan: what data, interviews, and analyses are required; in what order; by whom
Issue tree: breaking the problem into MECE sub-questions that collectively answer the main question
Expectation setting: client and team agreement on deliverables, timeline, and communication cadence
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team presents their charter (10 minutes) and issue tree, followed by structured faculty and peer critique. Focus: is the problem statement specific and answerable? Is the hypothesis testable? Does the issue tree cover all possibilities without overlap?

review.value: Do not boil the ocean. Start with a hypothesis about the answer and design your analysis to test it. If the analysis disproves the hypothesis, form a new one. This is faster than open-ended exploration and produces clearer recommendations

When you decompose a problem, every sub-question should be mutually exclusive (no overlap) and collectively exhaustive (nothing missing). MECE is not just a discipline; it is how you ensure no important angle is missed

class_schedule.topic: Milestone 2 Review: Industry, Competitive, and Internal Diagnosis
learning_objectives: Present industry and competitive analysis using Porter's Five Forces and strategic group mapping
Present internal diagnosis using value chain, VRIO, or similar frameworks
Show evidence base with primary and secondary data
readings: Barney, 'Firm Resources and Sustained Competitive Advantage' (VRIO framework)
Kaplan and Norton, Balanced Scorecard, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: With your charter approved, the diagnostic phase produces the evidence base for recommendations. Rigorous diagnosis is what distinguishes consulting from opinion. Whatever strategy you recommend in Milestone 3 must be grounded in this diagnostic work.
key_concepts: Porter's Five Forces: threat of entry, supplier power, buyer power, threat of substitutes, industry rivalry
Strategic group mapping: clustering competitors by dimensions that matter to customers
Value chain analysis: primary and support activities, identifying sources of cost advantage or differentiation
VRIO framework: evaluating resources by Value, Rarity, Imitability, and Organization
Primary research: interviews with customers, employees, suppliers, industry experts
Secondary research: market reports, financial filings, trade press, academic literature
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team presents (15 minutes) their industry analysis, competitive analysis, and internal diagnosis, with synthesized 'so-what' takeaways connecting the evidence to the central problem. Faculty panel critiques the rigor of the evidence and the linkage to the problem.
setup_requirements: Industry analysis document with Five Forces
Competitive analysis with strategic group map
Internal diagnosis with value chain or VRIO
Evidence appendix with sources and interview notes

review.value: Every claim in your final deliverable must be supported by specific evidence. 'We believe' without data is opinion; 'data shows' with citation is consulting. Build the evidence base before drafting conclusions
Analysis without implications is academic exercise. After every chart, interview, or financial ratio, ask 'so what does this mean for the client?' If there is no so-what, cut the analysis

class_schedule.topic: Milestone 3 Review: Strategic Alternatives and Evaluation
learning_objectives: Generate a comprehensive set of strategic alternatives grounded in the diagnosis
Evaluate alternatives against explicit criteria: strategic fit, financial impact, feasibility, risk
Present a preliminary recommendation with rationale
readings: Lafley and Martin, Playing to Win, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Good strategy requires considering options before committing to one. Teams that jump directly from diagnosis to a single recommendation miss alternatives that might better serve the client. This milestone requires you to generate at least three strategic alternatives, evaluate each rigorously, and defend your preliminary recommendation with explicit reasoning.
key_concepts: Strategic alternatives: 'where to play' and 'how to win' choices (Playing to Win framework)
Evaluation criteria: strategic fit with client capabilities, expected financial impact, implementation feasibility, risk profile
Weighted scoring matrix: formalizes the evaluation against criteria and weights
Decision justification: why this alternative, why not the others
Preliminary recommendation: the candidate answer going into financial modeling in milestone 4
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team presents its strategic alternatives, evaluation matrix, and preliminary recommendation. The faculty panel stress-tests the alternatives: are there options the team has not considered? Is the evaluation biased toward the team's preferred answer? Does the rationale actually connect the evidence to the choice?
setup_requirements: Evaluation matrix with explicit criteria and scoring

review.value: Weak alternative analysis generates straw men: obviously bad options that make the preferred choice look good. Strong alternative analysis generates genuine competing options, each plausible, and demonstrates why the selected one is best. Do the hard thinking

Implicit criteria hide biases. State your evaluation criteria explicitly, weight them, and score alternatives against them. This discipline also helps the client understand and agree with your recommendation rather than having to trust it blindly

class_schedule.topic: Milestone 4 Review: Financial Model and Implementation Plan
learning_objectives: Present a three-statement financial model projecting the recommendation's impact
Conduct sensitivity analysis on key assumptions
Present an implementation roadmap with phases, milestones, and change management considerations
readings: Pignataro, Financial Modeling and Valuation, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: A recommendation without financial grounding is speculation. The financial model converts your strategic logic into quantified expected outcomes, enabling the client to evaluate the recommendation against business reality. Sensitivity analysis honestly discloses which assumptions most affect the outcome. The implementation roadmap translates strategy into action.
key_concepts: Three-statement model: income statement, balance sheet, cash flow statement linked through accounting identities
Assumption discipline: every line item driven by an explicit, traceable assumption
Scenario analysis: base, upside, downside with explicit assumption changes
Sensitivity analysis: varying one assumption at a time to find drivers of outcome
NPV, IRR, payback period: evaluation metrics for investment decisions
Implementation roadmap: phased actions with owners, dates, dependencies, milestones
Change management: Kotter's 8 steps, stakeholder mapping, quick wins
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team presents its financial model and walks through the assumption base. Faculty panel tests assumptions: are they grounded in the diagnostic evidence? Are they realistic? What happens when key assumptions change? The implementation roadmap is evaluated for practicality and change management awareness.
setup_requirements: Financial model (Excel) with linked three statements

review.value: Clients trust your financial model to the extent they can see and agree with your assumptions. Every assumption should be defended with a source or reasoning; assumptions in footnotes outrank answers in the summary

Every financial model has sensitive assumptions. Identifying them and presenting their impact is not weakness; it is honesty. Clients respect consultants who disclose uncertainty and distrust those who hide it

learning_objectives: Deliver a final consulting report with recommendations, supporting analysis, and implementation plan
Present recommendations to faculty panel (and client if available) in an executive briefing format
Complete a postmortem and package the engagement as a portfolio artifact
readings: Revisiting Minto, The Pyramid Principle (executive summaries)
Example final reports from previous capstones
Career preparation: framing the capstone in interviews

lecture_notes.introduction: The final deliverable is what your client (or future employer) will actually see. Package it carefully: a polished consulting report, an executive presentation, and a thoughtful postmortem. Together these become cornerstone portfolio pieces and interview material for your career launch.
key_concepts: Consulting report structure: executive summary, context, diagnosis, alternatives, recommendation, implementation, appendices
Pyramid principle in writing: answer-first structure throughout the document
Executive presentation: 15-20 slides, minimum text, maximum visual clarity, rehearsed delivery
Postmortem format: what went right, what went wrong, what the team would do differently
Individual reflection: each member documents specific contributions and learning
Portfolio packaging: NDA-aware redaction if applicable, presentation-ready format
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team presents a 20-minute executive briefing: 15 minutes presenting the full recommendation with key slides, 5 minutes Q&A. Faculty panel (and client observers if available) evaluate the professionalism, analytical rigor, and persuasiveness of the final deliverable.

review.value: Employers value self-awareness. The most valuable postmortems honestly describe mistakes and what was learned, not retrospectively justified perfection. Be specific about what you would do differently with more experience

When presenting your capstone in interviews, state your specific role and contributions, the hardest analytical problem you personally solved, the recommendation and its expected impact, and what you would do differently. This narrative transforms a school project into professional credibility

major_assignments.title: Milestone 1: Project Charter and Diagnostic Plan
major_assignments.description: Produce a consulting engagement charter with client context, problem statement, working hypothesis, MECE issue tree, diagnostic plan, and data acquisition strategy.
objectives: Define a specific, answerable client problem
State a testable working hypothesis
Decompose the problem into a MECE issue tree
Plan the diagnostic work with specific analyses and data sources
deliverables: Project charter (6-8 pages) with client background, problem statement, scope, working hypothesis, issue tree, and timeline
Diagnostic plan specifying analyses, data sources, interview list, and owners for each analytical task
Team charter with roles, communication protocols, decision-making norms
10-minute team presentation with Q&A

criteria: Problem statement is specific and answerable in 15 weeks
Working hypothesis is testable and falsifiable
Scope is appropriate for team size and timeline
Client context is accurately captured
Issue tree is MECE (mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive)
Sub-questions collectively answer the main question
Analytical logic is clear and defensible
Hypothesis is connected to testable sub-questions
Diagnostic plan is realistic and detailed
Data sources and interview targets are identified
Team roles leverage individual strengths
Timeline is realistic with buffer
Specific, answerable problem with testable hypothesis
MECE issue tree with clear analytical logic
Realistic diagnostic plan with data sources
Strong team organization with defined roles

major_assignments.title: Milestone 2: Industry, Competitive, and Internal Diagnosis
major_assignments.description: Deliver a rigorous diagnostic analysis using consulting frameworks (Porter's Five Forces, strategic group mapping, value chain, VRIO) supported by primary and secondary research.
objectives: Analyze the industry structure and dynamics
Map the competitive landscape and the client's position
Diagnose internal capabilities and constraints
Build an evidence base supporting the analysis
deliverables: Industry analysis document with Porter's Five Forces and industry drivers
Competitive analysis with strategic group map and client positioning
Internal diagnosis with value chain or VRIO
Evidence appendix with sources, interview notes, and data tables
Synthesized 'so-what' summary connecting diagnosis to the central problem

criteria: Frameworks are applied correctly, not mechanically
Analysis goes beyond textbook definitions to client-specific insights
Primary research supplements secondary research
Evidence is traceable and credible
Diagnosis clearly connects to the central problem
'So-what' implications are stated explicitly
Findings are prioritized by impact on the problem
Synthesis goes beyond listing to identifying patterns
Documents are polished and professionally formatted
Charts and visuals communicate clearly
Citations and sources are professionally managed
Presentation is well-rehearsed
Rigorous application of consulting frameworks
Clear linkage of diagnosis to the central problem
Strong evidence base with primary and secondary research
Professional deliverables suitable for a client audience

major_assignments.title: Milestone 3: Strategic Alternatives and Evaluation
major_assignments.description: Generate and evaluate at least three strategic alternatives against explicit criteria, arriving at a defensible preliminary recommendation.
objectives: Generate genuine alternatives grounded in the diagnosis
Define explicit evaluation criteria with weights
Score alternatives rigorously and defend the scoring
Arrive at a preliminary recommendation with clear rationale
deliverables: Strategic alternatives document describing 3-5 options, their logic, and required capabilities
Evaluation matrix with weighted criteria and scores for each alternative
Sensitivity check: how robust is the recommendation to scoring variation
Preliminary recommendation with rationale (5-7 pages)

criteria: Alternatives are genuine and substantively different
Each alternative is described with enough detail to evaluate
Alternatives are grounded in the diagnostic findings
Required capabilities and resources are identified
Criteria are explicit and relevant to the client
Weights reflect strategic priorities
Scoring is evidence-based, not biased toward preferred option
Sensitivity check demonstrates robustness
Recommendation follows from the evaluation
Rationale is clear and persuasive
Trade-offs are acknowledged honestly
Implementation implications are noted
Substantive alternatives representing genuine choices
Rigorous evaluation against explicit criteria
Defensible recommendation with clear rationale
Honest acknowledgment of trade-offs

major_assignments.title: Milestone 4: Financial Model and Implementation Plan
major_assignments.description: Build a three-statement financial model quantifying the recommendation's impact, conduct sensitivity and scenario analysis, and produce an implementation roadmap with change management considerations.
objectives: Build a complete, assumption-driven financial model
Conduct scenario and sensitivity analysis
Translate the strategy into a phased implementation plan
Address change management and stakeholder risks
deliverables: Three-statement financial model in Excel with linked income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow
Assumption document describing each assumption and its source
Scenario analysis (base, upside, downside) with clear assumption changes
Sensitivity analysis on at least five key drivers
Implementation roadmap with phases, milestones, owners, dependencies
Change management plan addressing stakeholders, communication, and risks

criteria: Model is technically correct with no formula errors
Three statements are linked through accounting identities
Assumptions are traceable and defensible
Model is clean, navigable, and professionally formatted
Scenarios reflect meaningful strategic variations
Sensitivity analysis identifies true drivers
NPV, IRR, and payback period calculations are correct
Results are interpreted clearly and connected to recommendation
Roadmap is realistic with sequenced phases
Change management plan addresses resistance and alignment
Stakeholder mapping is thorough
Quick wins and long-term outcomes are both identified
Risk mitigation is specific and actionable
Technically correct, assumption-driven financial model
Rigorous sensitivity and scenario analysis
Practical implementation roadmap with change management
Quantified impact supporting the recommendation

major_assignments.title: Milestone 5: Final Recommendations, Presentation, and Postmortem
major_assignments.description: Deliver a polished final consulting report, an executive presentation to faculty (and client if available), and a professional postmortem. Package the engagement for portfolio use.
objectives: Produce a final report suitable for a client audience
Deliver a persuasive executive presentation
Reflect honestly on what worked and what did not
Document individual contributions and growth
deliverables: Final consulting report (25-35 pages) with executive summary, context, diagnosis, alternatives, recommendation, financial model, and implementation plan
Executive presentation (15-20 slides) following the pyramid principle
Postmortem document (8-10 pages) analyzing successes, failures, and lessons learned
Individual reflection essays (2-3 pages each) on specific contributions and growth
Public presentation (25 minutes: 15-minute executive briefing plus 10-minute postmortem) with faculty Q&A
Portfolio-ready packaging of all materials

criteria: Recommendation is defensible and grounded in evidence
Report is polished and professionally written
Financial model supports the recommendation
Implementation plan is practical
Deliverables suit a client audience
Honest analysis of successes and failures
Root causes identified, not just symptoms
Lessons are specific and actionable
Writing is professional and reflective
Executive briefing is persuasive and well-paced
Visual aids support the narrative
All team members contribute meaningfully
Q&A responses demonstrate depth
Honest assessment of personal contributions
Specific examples of challenges overcome
Connection to career development goals
Self-awareness about growth areas
Polished final consulting deliverable meeting professional standards
Insightful postmortem demonstrating genuine learning
Persuasive executive presentation to faculty and client
Thoughtful individual reflection on growth and contributions

milestone_deliverables.description: Five milestone submissions tracking the consulting engagement from charter through final recommendation

final_product.description: Quality and rigor of the final consulting report and financial model

postmortem.description: Written analysis of the engagement process and lessons learned

peer_evaluation.description: Team contribution assessment from all team members

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all milestone reviews, client meetings, and the final presentation. Missing a milestone review without prior arrangement results in a zero for that milestone. Client meetings require full team presence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Milestone deadlines are firm. Late submissions are penalized 15% per day. The final deliverable deadline is absolute. Client-facing deliverables must never be late.
course_policies.collaboration: This is a required team project. All team members must contribute substantively. Peer evaluations adjust individual grades. Disagreements within teams should be raised early with faculty, not allowed to fester.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All analysis must be original team work or properly attributed. Using prior capstone work, published consulting reports, or generative AI without disclosure constitutes plagiarism. Frameworks from textbooks are expected but should be cited appropriately.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Teams must maintain a shared workspace (Notion, Google Drive, or equivalent), a financial modeling tool (Excel with Solver, or Google Sheets), and a presentation tool. Client-facing deliverables must be polished and exported as PDF. NDA-compliance is required for any engagement with a real client.
